questions.question: Define the node structure used for representing a polynomial using a linked list. List the three components stored in each node.
keyPoints: Each node contains coefficient (coeff) - the numeric multiplier
Each node contains exponent (expo) - the power of the variable
Each node contains next pointer - pointer to the next term
Terms are stored in decreasing order of exponents

questions.question: Explain briefly why linked list representation is preferred over array representation for sparse polynomials. Give a suitable example.
keyPoints: Sparse polynomial has most coefficients as zero
For polynomial 3x^1000 + 2x + 7, array needs 1001 entries but linked list needs only 3 nodes
Linked list stores only non-zero terms, reducing memory waste
Array representation wastes memory with O(d) space where d is maximum degree

questions.question: Explain the algorithm for adding two polynomials represented using linked lists. Discuss how the algorithm handles terms with equal exponents and when one polynomial gets exhausted.
keyPoints: Both polynomials are stored in decreasing exponent order
Compare exponents of current nodes from both polynomials
If exponents are unequal, copy the term with larger exponent to result
If exponents are equal, add coefficients and create node only if sum is non-zero
When one list is exhausted, copy all remaining terms from the other list
Time complexity is O(m + n) where m and n are number of terms

questions.question: Compare and contrast array representation and linked list representation of polynomials with respect to memory usage, access time, and suitability for sparse and dense polynomials.
keyPoints: Array uses index as exponent and value as coefficient - O(d) memory where d is maximum degree
Linked list stores only non-zero terms - O(n) memory where n is number of non-zero terms
Array provides O(1) access to coefficient of any exponent by direct indexing
Linked list requires O(n) traversal to access a specific exponent
Linked list is ideal for sparse polynomials (waste less memory)
Array is better for dense polynomials (no pointer overhead)
Addition in array is O(max(d1,d2)) while in linked list is O(n1+n2)

questions.question: Write an algorithm to multiply two polynomials represented using linked lists. Also mention how like terms are combined and state the time complexity.
keyPoints: For each term in first polynomial, iterate through all terms of second polynomial
New coefficient = product of coefficients of the two terms
New exponent = sum of exponents of the two terms
Insert each product term into result using insertTerm which automatically combines like exponents
Time complexity is O(m × n × r) where m,n are input terms and r is result terms
Space complexity is O(m × n) in worst case for result polynomial
InsertTerm function handles zero coefficient terms by not creating nodes
